content.ci-cd-pipeline:
CI/CD Pipeline

Continuous Integration and Continuous Deployment (CI/CD) automates the process of testing, building, and deploying applications. This guide walks through setting up a robust pipeline using GitHub Actions.

Pipeline Stages

A typical CI/CD pipeline consists of:

  1. Source: Triggered by code push or pull request
  2. Build: Compile code and create artifacts
  3. Test: Run unit, integration, and linting checks
  4. Deploy: Push artifacts to staging/production

GitHub Actions Example

name: CI/CD Pipeline
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Run tests
        run: npm test

  deploy:
    needs: test
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Deploy to production
        run: echo "Deploying..."

Best Practices

  • Fast Feedback: Keep build times under 10 minutes
  • Idempotent Builds: Same commit → same artifact every time
  • Secret Management: Use repository secrets, never hardcode credentials
  • Environment Parity: Dev, staging, and production environments should match
  • Rollback Strategy: Always have a plan to revert a deployment
content.docker-basics:
Docker Basics

Docker is a platform for developing, shipping, and running applications inside lightweight, portable containers. This article covers the basics you need to get started.

What are Containers?

Containers are lightweight, standalone packages that include everything needed to run a piece of software — code, runtime, system tools, libraries, and settings. Unlike virtual machines, containers share the host operating system kernel.

Key Concepts

  • Images: Read-only templates used to create containers
  • Containers: Runnable instances of images
  • Dockerfile: A text file with instructions to build an image
  • Registry: A repository for storing and sharing images (e.g., Docker Hub)
  • Volumes: Persistent storage for container data

Basic Commands

docker pull nginx          # Download an image
docker build -t my-app .   # Build an image from a Dockerfile
docker run -d -p 80:80 nginx  # Run a container
docker ps                   # List running containers
docker stop <container_id>  # Stop a container
docker logs <container_id>  # View container logs
content.linux-essentials:
Linux Essentials

Linux proficiency is a core skill for any DevOps engineer. This guide covers the essential commands and concepts you need to navigate and manage Linux systems effectively.

File System Navigation

ls -la           # List files with details
cd /path/to/dir  # Change directory
pwd              # Print working directory
find / -name "*.log"  # Search for files
du -sh *         # Check directory sizes

Process Management

ps aux           # List all running processes
top              # Real-time process monitoring
kill -9 <PID>    # Force kill a process
systemctl start <service>  # Manage systemd services
journalctl -u <service>    # View service logs

Permissions

chmod 755 file   # Set file permissions
chown user:group file  # Change file owner
umask            # View/set default permissions

Networking

ss -tulpn        # List listening ports
curl -v http://example.com  # HTTP requests
ping <host>      # Test connectivity
traceroute <host>  # Trace network path
content.llm-fine-tuning:
LLM Fine-Tuning Guide

Fine-tuning adapts a pre-trained large language model to perform specific tasks or understand domain-specific knowledge. This guide covers the end-to-end process from data preparation to deployment.

When to Fine-Tune

Consider fine-tuning when:

  • Domain Specialization: You need the model to understand technical jargon (legal, medical, code)
  • Task Specificity: Standard prompting doesn't achieve the desired output format or quality
  • Data Privacy: You cannot send proprietary data through third-party API endpoints
  • Latency: On-device or self-hosted inference is required

Data Preparation

Raw Data → Clean → Format → Validate → Split (80/10/10)
  • Collect high-quality, task-specific examples (min 500-1000 for noticeable improvements)
  • Format as instruction-response pairs or conversation turns
  • Deduplicate and filter low-quality samples
  • Include edge cases and failure scenarios

Fine-Tuning Techniques

Full Fine-Tuning

  • Updates all model parameters
  • Requires significant compute (multiple GPUs)
  • Best for major domain adaptation

LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation)

  • Adds trainable rank-decomposition matrices to attention layers
  • Reduces trainable parameters by 10,000x
  • Memory efficient — fine-tune on a single consumer GPU
  • Easy to swap adapters without full model reload

QLoRA

  • Quantizes the base model to 4-bit precision
  • Adds LoRA adapters on top
  • Fine-tune 65B parameter models on a single 48GB GPU
  • Negligible quality loss compared to full fine-tuning

Hyperparameters

Parameter Typical Range Notes
Learning Rate 1e-5 to 5e-4 Lower for full fine-tuning
Batch Size 4-64 Larger batches with gradient accum
Epochs 1-5 Stop when eval loss plateaus
LoRA Rank (r) 8-64 Higher = more capacity
LoRA Alpha 16-128 Usually 2x the rank
Warmup Steps 50-200 Stabilizes initial training

Evaluation

  • Perplexity: Measure of prediction quality on held-out data
  • Human Evaluation: Rate outputs for relevance, accuracy, and tone
  • Task-Specific Metrics: BLEU for translation, ROUGE for summarization, F1 for classification
  • A/B Testing: Compare fine-tuned vs. base model on real user queries

Deployment

  • Export in GGUF format for CPU-friendly inference (llama.cpp)
  • Use vLLM or TGI for high-throughput GPU serving
  • Dockerize with model weights and inference server
  • Implement a feedback loop to collect real-world performance data
content.microservices-patterns:
Microservices Patterns

Microservices architecture decomposes an application into independently deployable services that communicate over a network. This guide covers the essential patterns you need to build robust microservice-based systems.

Decomposition Patterns

  • Decompose by Business Capability: Align services with business functions (inventory, billing, shipping)
  • Decompose by Subdomain: Use Domain-Driven Design bounded contexts to define service boundaries
  • Strangler Fig: Incrementally replace monolithic components with microservices

Communication Patterns

Service A ──HTTP/REST──→ Service B
    │                        │
    │                        │
    └────Message Queue───────┘
  • Synchronous: REST, gRPC for request-response interactions
  • Asynchronous: Message brokers (Kafka, RabbitMQ) for event-driven communication
  • API Gateway: Single entry point for routing, auth, rate limiting

Data Management

  • Database per Service: Each service owns its data store — prevents tight coupling
  • Saga Pattern: Distributed transactions via choreography or orchestration
  • CQRS: Separate read and write models for query/command optimization
  • Event Sourcing: Persist state changes as an append-only event log

Observability

  • Distributed Tracing: Trace requests across service boundaries (Jaeger, Zipkin)
  • Centralized Logging: Aggregate logs from all services (ELK stack)
  • Health Checks: /health and /ready endpoints for Kubernetes probes

Deployment

  • Containerization: Docker packages each service with its dependencies
  • Orchestration: Kubernetes manages service deployment, scaling, and healing
  • Service Mesh: Istio/Linkerd handle service-to-service communication, retries, and circuit breaking

Best Practices

  • Design for failure — use circuit breakers, retries, and timeouts
  • Implement CI/CD pipelines per service for independent deployments
  • Version APIs explicitly (e.g., /v1/orders, /v2/orders)
  • Use infrastructure as code for reproducible environments
